summary: The 37th President of the United States (1969-1974), a California Congressman and Senator, and Dwight Eisenhower's Vice President, nicknamed "Tricky Dick." Clues identify him through his early Red-baiting campaigns against Jerry Voorhis and Helen Gahagan Douglas, the 1952 "Checkers Speech," the 1959 Kitchen Debate with Nikita Khrushchev, his loss to John F. Kennedy in the first televised presidential debates, his presidency's domestic environmental legislation (the EPA, Clean Air and Clean Water Acts), his landmark 1972 visit to China, his conduct of the Vietnam War, and above all the Watergate scandal, the White House tapes, and his resignation -- the only U.S. president to resign -- followed by Gerald Ford's pardon.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Giveaway clues identify this man as the 37th President of the United States, a California Congressman and Senator before that, Dwight Eisenhower's Vice President, and the only U.S. president to resign, having done so amid the Watergate scandal. He was widely nicknamed "Tricky Dick."

clues.clue: This man was the 37th President of the United States, a California Congressman and Senator, and Dwight Eisenhower's Vice President, who resigned as president amid the Watergate scandal -- the only U.S. president to do so
examples: name this politician who resigned as president to avoid being impeached for the Watergate scandal.
For 10 points, identify this California Congressman and Senator who later served as President, nicknamed "Tricky Dick."
name this US president who resigned after the Watergate scandal.

clues.clue: This man was nicknamed "Tricky Dick"
examples: prompt on "Tricky Dick"
name these opponents who, during the 1950 California Senate election, called each other "Tricky Dick" and "The Pink Lady."

works.name: Early Career: Congress, the Hiss Case, and Red-Baiting Campaigns
works.description: Before the presidency, this man built a career in California politics through aggressive anticommunist campaigning: he unseated Jerry Voorhis for Congress, doggedly pursued accused Soviet spy Alger Hiss, and won a bitter 1950 Senate race against Helen Gahagan Douglas by branding her "pink right down to her underwear" on the notorious "Pink Sheet." He also weathered a 1958 stoning of his car in Caracas and the 1952 "Checkers Speech," in which he denied wrongdoing over a secret expense fund and vowed to keep his family's cocker spaniel, Checkers.

clues.clue: This politician reportedly pioneered push polling in his 1946 campaign against Jerry Voorhis by calling Democratic voters and asking, "Did you know Jerry Voorhis is a Communist?"
examples: This politician reportedly pioneered push polling by calling Democrat voters and asking, "Did you know Jerry Voorhis is a Communist?"

clues.clue: As a Congressman, this man doggedly pursued accused Soviet spy Alger Hiss, and co-sponsored a bill with Karl Mundt that would have required Communist organizations to register with a "Subversive Activities Control Board"
examples: doggedly pursued Alger Hiss, and also made Elvis Presley an honorary member of the DEA.
With Karl Mundt, this politician co-sponsored a bill that would have required Communist organizations to be registered with a "Subversive Activities Control Board."

clues.clue: This man won the bitter 1950 California Senate race against Helen Gahagan Douglas by branding her "pink right down to her underwear" on a "Pink Sheet" comparing her voting record to that of Vito Marcantonio; the two nicknamed each other "Tricky Dick" and "The Pink Lady"
examples: This person smeared Helen Douglas as a Communist sympathiser with the Pink Sheet.
compared Helen Gahagan Douglas's voting record to that of Vito Marcantonio and made the accusation that she was "pink right down to her underwear."
name these opponents who, during the 1950 California Senate election, called each other "Tricky Dick" and "The Pink Lady."

clues.clue: During that 1950 Senate campaign, this man's manager Murray Chotiner colored the "Pink Sheet"; the campaign allegedly wanted a bare-chested "STRIPPED FOR ACTION" photo of him, and he was caught ripping up a sign in Chinese alleging a Howard Hughes loan to his brother
examples: This man allegedly wanted to use a photograph of himself bare-chested standing with a pith helmet titled "STRIPPED FOR ACTION"... He was caught on camera ripping up a sign in Chinese that allegedly discussed a loan Howard Hughes made to his brother.

clues.clue: In the 1952 "Checkers Speech," this man, then Eisenhower's running mate, denied receiving $18,000 in unreported gifts, said his wife did not own a mink coat, and vowed to keep the family's gift cocker spaniel, Checkers, rather than resign from the ticket
examples: this politician gave a televised speech stating he would keep a cocker spaniel named Checkers and not resign as Eisenhower's running mate.
this politician denied that his wife owned a mink coat in that speech named for a cocker spaniel given to his daughter as a gift.
The Checkers Speech was given by this man, who as Vice President for Dwight Eisenhower got into the Kitchen Debate with Nikita Khrushchev.

clues.clue: In 1958, while on a visit to Caracas, Venezuela, this man's car was attacked by a mob, an event that shaped his later promotion of Vernon Walters; he also gave a 1958 speech in Montevideo, Uruguay that sparked riots
examples: While on a visit to Caracas, this person's car was attacked, an event which shaped their promotions of Vernon Walters.
Nixon gave a 1958 speech about US foreign policy in this city, which sparked subsequent riots.

clues.clue: This man was the founder-president of the ill-fated frozen orange juice company Citri-Frost, and founded the Orthogonian Society at Whittier College (with Albert Upton) for students denied entry to the elite Franklin Society
examples: This person was the founder-president of the ill-fated frozen orange juice company Citri-Frost.
Denied a spot in Whittier College's elite Franklin Society, this man founded the Orthogonian Society for students from poorer backgrounds like himself.

clues.clue: This man lost the 1962 California gubernatorial election to Pat Brown, giving a "last press conference" in which he said reporters "won't have Nixon to kick around anymore"
examples: This politician gave his "last press conference" by stating "you don't have [this politician] to kick around anymore" after losing a 1962 election to Pat Brown.

works.name: The Vice Presidency, the Kitchen Debate, and the 1960 Election
works.description: As Eisenhower's Vice President, this man sparred with Soviet leader Nikita Khrushchev over capitalism and communism in the televised "Kitchen Debate" at a 1959 Moscow exhibition. In 1960 he faced John F. Kennedy in the first televised U.S. presidential debates, where his sickly appearance -- the result of a knee injury from a car door -- famously cost him the votes of TV viewers even as radio listeners judged him the winner.

clues.clue: As Vice President, this man argued with Soviet Premier Nikita Khrushchev over American versus Soviet living standards in the 1959 "Kitchen Debate" at the American National Exhibition in Moscow, where Khrushchev sampled Pepsi (promoted by Donald Kendall)
examples: Before becoming president, this man argued with Khrushchev about American versus Soviet lifestyles in the Kitchen Debate.
as Vice President for Dwight Eisenhower got into the Kitchen Debate with Nikita Khrushchev.

clues.clue: This man debated John F. Kennedy in the first televised U.S. presidential debates in 1960; radio listeners judged this man the winner, while TV viewers preferred the more telegenic Kennedy
examples: name these first televised events of their kind, which played a huge role in the victory of a certain Catholic in the 1960 presidential election.
Radio listeners are famously said to have awarded the first of these events to that man, while those who watched on television preferred his younger counterpart.
name these verbal contests between a Massachusetts Senator and Eisenhower's Vice President in advance of the 1960 Presidential election.

clues.clue: Before the first debate, this man injured his knee on a car door, leading to a hospital stay and infection that, combined with weight loss and a five-o'clock shadow, left him looking haggard and sweaty on camera
examples: Before one debate, this politician hit his knee on a car door and had to be hospitalized.
The other participant in these events bashed his knee on a car door in North Carolina, which became a severe infection, resulting in him losing 20 pounds and appearing haggard.

clues.clue: The third of these 1960 debates focused on the Taiwanese-controlled islands of Quemoy and Matsu
examples: These two politicians were placed in identical studios to debate whether American troops should prevent the islands of Quemoy and Matsu from falling into enemy hands.
The second and third of these events centered on Quemoy and Matsu, two islands that were contested between Taiwan and China.

clues.clue: This man and Kennedy, then freshman House representatives, had earlier shared a sleeping car on a train back to Washington after debating in McKeesport, Pennsylvania in front of steel union members over the Taft-Hartley Act
examples: These two freshman House representatives shared a sleeping car room on the train back to Washington after squaring off in McKeesport for a debate in front of steel union members incensed by the Taft-Hartley Act.

works.name: Domestic Policy and the Environment
works.description: As president, this man signed a wave of environmental legislation -- creating the Environmental Protection Agency and signing the Endangered Species Act, while Congress overrode his vetoes of the Clean Air and Clean Water Acts -- alongside major economic interventions like wage-price controls and taking the U.S. off the gold standard, and an unsuccessful push for a guaranteed-income Family Assistance Plan.

clues.clue: This president signed the Endangered Species Act and created the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), partly inspired by the first Earth Day and teach-ins organized with UAW backing
examples: name this president who signed the Endangered Species Act and created the Environmental Protection Agency.
The celebration of the first Earth Day inspired this president to form a new governmental body.
The EPA was established under this president, who visited Mao Zedong in China in 1972.

clues.clue: Congress overrode this president's veto of the Clean Air Act (championed by Edmund Muskie, setting national air quality standards) and his veto of the Clean Water Act (motivated by the Cuyahoga River Fire)
examples: Despite this president's veto, Congress passed an act supported by Edmund Muskie establishing national air quality standards for various pollutants.
Senators overrode this president's veto of the Clean Water Act, a bill motivated by the Cuyahoga River Fire.

clues.clue: His administration temporarily banned offshore drilling in federal waters after the Santa Barbara oil spill, and Interior Secretary Walter Hickel tightened offshore drilling regulations
examples: This president's administration temporarily banned offshore drilling in federal waters after the (*) Santa Barbara oil spill.
This president's Secretary of the Interior, Walter Hickel, tightened offshore drilling regulations following the Santa Barbara Oil Spill.

clues.clue: Congress failed to pass this president's proposed Family Assistance Plan, a guaranteed-income measure that would have given a family of four $1,600 per year and passed the House but failed in the Senate
examples: Congress failed to pass this politician's proposal for universal basic income called the Family Assistance Plan.
This US President proposed a form of basic income he called the Family Assistance Plan, or FAP, under which a family of four would receive $1,600 per year.

clues.clue: This president endorsed the Equal Rights Amendment after Martha Griffiths reintroduced it to Congress
examples: After Martha Griffiths reintroduced it to Congress, this president endorsed the Equal Rights Amendment.

clues.clue: This president imposed wage-price controls that set a maximum price on beef sales but not on corn and wheat cattle feed, causing a nationwide beef shortage
examples: During this man's administration, he set a maximum price on beef sales but did not control prices on corn and wheat cattle feed, causing a nationwide beef shortage.

clues.clue: As part of his namesake economic "Shock," this president took the United States off the gold standard and modified exchange rates in the Smithsonian Agreement
examples: As part of his namesake "shock", he took the US off the gold standard.
His administration modified exchange rates in the Smithsonian Agreement.

clues.clue: During this president's tenure, the Soviets secretly bought $750 million worth of U.S. crops in the "Great Grain Robbery," and a uranium cartel formed that raised the price of uranium from $6 to $41 per pound
examples: During his tenure, the Soviets somewhat-secretly bought seven hundred and fifty million dollars' worth of crops from the US in the "Great Grain Robbery".
A uranium cartel that formed during this man's presidency caused the price of that commodity to increase from $6 to $41 per pound.

clues.clue: This president instituted a national speed limit in response to the 1973 oil crisis, and appointed his friend Don Kendall of Pepsi to chair the National Alliance of Businessmen
examples: name this president who instituted a national speed limit in response to the 1973 oil crisis.
he appointed Don Kendall, his buddy at Pepsi, to be chairman of the National Alliance of Businessmen.

clues.clue: This president's "New Federalism" policy shifted power to the states through general revenue sharing and non-earmarked block grants rather than categorical formula grants
examples: these polities received more general revenue sharing and non-earmarked block grants to reduce logrolling and pork barrel from categorical formula and project grants. (The first line describes features of Nixon's "new federalism.")

clues.clue: This president appointed conservative commentator William F. Buckley Jr. as a UN delegate, and was president during the Apollo 11 Moon landing
examples: This president appointed Buckley as a delegate to the United Nations. He was president during the Apollo 11 landings and the establishment of the EPA.

works.name: China, Detente, and Foreign Policy
works.description: This president's most celebrated foreign policy achievement was his 1972 trip to China -- the first by a sitting U.S. president -- where he met Mao Zedong and Zhou Enlai and produced the Shanghai Communique, a landmark step toward normalizing U.S.-China relations that Henry Kissinger arranged through secret diplomacy.

clues.clue: In 1972, this president became the first sitting U.S. president to visit China, meeting Mao Zedong and shaking hands with Zhou Enlai; the trip was arranged through Henry Kissinger's secret meetings and facilitated by "ping-pong diplomacy" the year before
examples: identify this 1972 event in which the 37th president of the US traveled to the world's most populous country.
It was organized through a series of secret meetings held by Henry Kissinger.
This event was facilitated by an exchange in which Tim Boggan and ten other table tennis players participated.

clues.clue: Journalist Max Frankel won a Pulitzer Prize for his coverage of this trip, during which aide Winston Lord was cropped out of photographs to avoid embarrassing a high-ranking official (arranged partly through Yahya Khan's Pakistani government) who was excluded from a meeting
examples: Max Frankel won a Pulitzer Prize for his coverage of this event.
Winston Lord was cropped out of several photographs taken during it.

clues.clue: This trip produced the Shanghai Communique, the first of the "Three Communiques," a landmark step toward normalizing U.S.-China relations and an example of Kissinger's "constructive ambiguity," opposing "hegemony and power politics"
examples: This event resulted in the issuing of the first of the Three Communiques.
The Shanghai Communique was issued during this president's historic diplomatic trip to China.

clues.clue: One politician called this trip the "week that changed the world"; the president was accompanied by Secretary of State William Rogers
examples: One politician called this event the "Week that changed the world", and that figure was accompanied by William Rogers.

works.name: The Vietnam War
works.description: This president's Vietnam War policy combined a 1968 back-channel effort to sabotage Lyndon Johnson's Paris peace talks with his later strategy as president of "Vietnamization" and appeals to the "Silent Majority," punctuated by the secret invasion of Cambodia, an impulsive late-night visit to protesting students at the Lincoln Memorial, and the massive May Day protests of 1971.

clues.clue: As president-elect in 1968, this man secretly used Anna Chennault as an intermediary to urge South Vietnamese President Nguyen Van Thieu to stall the Paris peace talks until after the election, a scheme partially assisted by Senator John Tower and detailed in Walt Rostow's "X-memorandum"
examples: This action was carried out using Anna Chennault as an intermediary... This action was carried out by coordination with Nguyen van Thieu, who suggested that any settlement with the Viet Cong should be postponed until the 1968 Presidential election.

clues.clue: In a 1969 speech, this president appealed to the "Silent Majority" -- Americans who did not actively oppose the war -- while outlining his policy of "Vietnamization," increasing the role of South Vietnamese forces and American helicopters
examples: This is the president who talked about his plans for eventual peace in Vietnam to the silent majority. He increased the participation of South Vietnamese in the war in his policy of Vietnamization.
Nixon's policy of reducing American presence in Vietnam through "Vietnamization" included an increased support role for these things.

clues.clue: This president ordered the 1970 invasion of Cambodia, targeting Viet Cong hideouts there
examples: The protesters Nixon conversed with were demonstrating against the invasion of this country.

clues.clue: In the early hours of May 9, 1970, this president impulsively visited the Lincoln Memorial to speak with student protesters, worrying his bodyguards; Chief of Staff H.R. Haldeman called it the "weirdest day so far"
examples: Identify this impulsive trip taken in the early hours of May 9, 1970 by Richard Nixon, who worried his bodyguards by insisting on speaking to the young student protestors he met along the way.

clues.clue: A massive 1971 May Day demonstration in Washington, D.C. protesting this president's actions in Vietnam resulted in over 12,000 arrests, the largest mass arrest in U.S. history
examples: Another "May Day" demonstration took place in 1971, in Washington, D.C.. Over 12,000 people were arrested at that demonstration, the largest mass arrest in history, which protested the actions of this U.S. president in Vietnam.

clues.clue: This president ordered Operation Giant Lance based on his self-described "madman theory," as he related to Haldeman, having admired strategist Herman Kahn's game-theoretic Cold War writings
examples: This U.S. president loved Herman Kahn, and issued the order for Operation Giant Lance based on what he called his "madman theory."

works.name: Watergate, the Tapes, and Resignation
works.description: The Watergate scandal, stemming from a break-in at the Democratic National Committee headquarters, ultimately destroyed this president's second term: secret White House tapes recorded him discussing a cover-up, his "enemies list" targeted critics, the "Saturday Night Massacre" saw him fire the special prosecutor investigating him, and the Supreme Court's ruling in U.S. v. Nixon forced him to surrender the tapes, leading to his resignation and Gerald Ford's subsequent pardon.

clues.clue: The scandal stemmed from a break-in at the Democratic National Committee headquarters in the Watergate complex; the "Smoking Gun" tape captured this president discussing covering up the break-in with Chief of Staff H.R. Haldeman
examples: The "Smoking Gun" portion of this record features Chief of Staff H.R. Haldeman discussing covering up a break-in of the Democratic National Committee headquarters with the President.
After this place came under the administration of NARA in 2005, it scrapped a sympathetic exhibit on an event that made Virgilio Gonzalez, Martha Mitchell, and the phrase "expletive deleted" famous.

clues.clue: This president compiled an "enemies list," later revealed by White House Counsel John Dean, that targeted critics including columnist Jack Anderson (whom Nixon aides allegedly tried to poison), historian Arthur Schlesinger Jr. (for The Imperial Presidency), journalist Daniel Schorr, and actor Paul Newman; cartoonist Herblock repeatedly depicted him climbing out of a sewer
examples: Daniel Schorr and Paul Newman were among the public figures on this politician's "enemies list" that John Dean revealed to the Select Committee on Presidential Campaign Activities.
This politician climbs out of a sewer in Herblock's cartoon "Here he comes now."
He claimed "I am not a crook" shortly before resigning due to the Watergate scandal.

clues.clue: At a press conference at Disney's Contemporary Resort, in the wake of the "Saturday Night Massacre" -- in which he had special prosecutor Archibald Cox fired, prompting Attorney General Elliot Richardson and Deputy AG William Ruckelshaus to resign rather than comply -- this president declared "I am not a crook"
examples: While at a press conference at Disney's contemporary resort, this politician proclaimed "I am not a crook" in the wake of the Saturday Night Massacre.
This man's attempts to subpoena the Watergate Tapes led to the Saturday Night Massacre, where both Elliot Richardson and William Ruckelshaus resigned rather than fire him.

clues.clue: This president had earlier offered to let the nearly deaf Senator John Stennis listen to and summarize the tapes for the special prosecutor, a compromise Cox's refusal of directly led to the Saturday Night Massacre
examples: Nixon had earlier offered to let this nearly deaf Senator listen to the tapes and summarize them for the Watergate special prosecutor. Archibald Cox's refusal led directly to the Saturday Night Massacre.

clues.clue: Leon Jaworski succeeded Cox as special prosecutor and won a Supreme Court ruling (U.S. v. Nixon, presided over by Chief Justice Warren Burger) forcing this president to surrender sixty-four taped conversations
examples: After Nixon succeeded in the Saturday Night Massacre by having Archibald Cox fired, this man took over as special prosecutor. He got the Supreme Court to force Nixon to turn over sixty-four taped conversations.
Nixon was forced to surrender the tapes after his claim of executive privilege was overturned in US v. Nixon That case was presided over by this Chief Justice, who also presided over Roe v. Wade.

clues.clue: This president's White House tapes contained a notorious 18.5-minute gap, blamed on secretary Rose Mary Woods's "Rose Mary Stretch" (or, per Alexander Haig, a "sinister force"); transcripts replaced profanity with the phrase "expletive deleted"; the tapes are now held in Yorba Linda, California
examples: an order to say "I can't recall" was changed to a suggestion, and terms like "damn" and "hell" were replaced with the phrase "expletive deleted".
an 18 1/2 minute gap due to an error by Rose Mary Woods. They are currently held in a facility in Yorba Linda.
An action known as the "Rose Mary Stretch" and the "sinister force" identified by Alexander Haig are two explanations for the missing eighteen and a half minutes.

clues.clue: This president's Chief of Staff, H.R. Haldeman, described himself as this man's "S.O.B." and spent 18 months in prison after resigning in 1973
examples: Haldeman called himself this man's "S.O.B." This president was pardoned by his successor Gerald Ford after becoming the only president to resign.
Identify this White House chief of staff who spent 18 months in prison after resigning in 1973.

clues.clue: This president resigned in August 1974 rather than face impeachment over the Watergate scandal, becoming the only U.S. president ever to resign
examples: name this president that resigned in the wake of the Watergate Scandal.
name this president who visited China before resigning in the midst of the Watergate scandal.

clues.clue: Gerald Ford, this man's successor, issued Proclamation 4311 pardoning him for all offenses committed between January 20, 1969 and August 9, 1974, in the name of preserving national "tranquility"
examples: This declaration applied to all actions undertaken between January 20th, 1969 and August 9th, 1974, and was intended to maintain the "tranquility" that a drawn-out trial and indictment would shatter.
identify this action in which the sitting US president absolved a previous president of all wrongdoing related to the Watergate Scandal.

clues.clue: The pardon was announced alongside an amnesty program for Vietnam War draft dodgers and led press secretary Jerald terHorst to resign in protest
examples: This action was announced along with an amnesty program for draft dodgers.
Jerald Franklin TerHorst resigned in protest of this declaration.

clues.clue: Critics called the pardon a modern-day "corrupt bargain," and it was one of the main reasons Ford lost the 1976 election to Jimmy Carter
examples: Critics argued that it was a modern-day "corrupt bargain", and it was one of the main reasons for its propagator's losing the election to Jimmy Carter.

works.name: Legacy and Cultural Depictions
works.description: This president's life has inspired an unusually large body of art and satire: John Adams's minimalist opera Nixon in China dramatizes his 1972 China trip (with his wife Pat Nixon as a major character), satirical novels by Thomas Pynchon, Robert Coover, and Kurt Vonnegut fictionalize him, Rick Perlstein's Nixonland chronicles his rise, and rubber Nixon masks became a recurring pop-culture prop. His presidential library sits in Yorba Linda, California.

clues.clue: This president's 1972 China trip inspired John Adams's 1987 minimalist opera Nixon in China (libretto by Alice Goodman, originally directed by Peter Sellars), which opens with the landing of the plane the Spirit of '76 and in which the title character sings "News has a kind of mystery"
examples: beginning with the landing of the plane the Spirit of '76, name this minimalist opera about a US president's international visit by John Adams.
The title character of this opera sings "News has a kind of mystery" when contemplating the effects of his actions.
This politician and his wife watch the ballet The Red Detachment of Women in a John Adams opera that fictionalizes his 1972 visit to China.

clues.clue: In Nixon in China, this president's wife Pat sings the aria "This is prophetic" at the Summer Palace after receiving a glass elephant figurine, and rushes onstage during a performance of the ballet The Red Detachment of Women to help a peasant girl she believes is being whipped to death
examples: While watching a performance, she rushes onstage to help a peasant girl seemingly being whipped to death.
name this character who earlier receives an elephant figurine, tours a school while reminiscing about when she was a teacher, and imagines world peace in the aria "This is Prophetic."
name this John Adams character, a First Lady who visits China.

clues.clue: Nixon in China's orchestral intermezzo "The Chairman Dances" (a "Foxtrot for Orchestra") depicts Mao Zedong and his wife dancing, and the opera ends with Zhou Enlai asking "How much of what we did was good?"
examples: Adams adapted this work from the third act of Nixon in China. This "Foxtrot for Orchestra" ends like a gramophone being wound down.
Name this opera which ends with Chou asking "How much of what we did was good?"

clues.clue: Real-life Pat Nixon personally aided relief efforts after the 1970 Great Peruvian Earthquake
examples: After the Great Peruvian Earthquake of 1970 triggered history's deadliest avalanche, the Velasco regime received the personal aid of this woman during their relief efforts.

clues.clue: This president is fictionalized in Thomas Pynchon's Gravity's Rainbow (whose final section is epigraphed by his one-word question "What?," and who appears as the night manager "Richard M. Zhlubb") and, in Gish Jen's 2022 story collection Thank You, Mr. Nixon, receives a letter from a girl named Tricia addressed to him "in hell"
examples: This person's one-word question "What?" serves as the epigraph for the final section of Gravity's Rainbow.
After meeting this person as a girl, Tricia writes a letter to him "in hell" in the first story of a 2022 collection by Gish Jen.

clues.clue: In Robert Coover's novel The Public Burning, this president narrates the story, urges a crowd to "drop your pants for America," and is sodomized by Uncle Sam amid the fictionalized execution of the Rosenbergs in Times Square
examples: This person urges a crowd to "drop [their] pants for America" and is sodomized by Uncle Sam in the novel The Public Burning.
Robert Coover's The Public Burning is narrated by, for 10 points, what vice president under Dwight Eisenhower?

clues.clue: This president appears as "Starbuck" in Kurt Vonnegut's novel Jailbird and, in Watchmen, serves five straight terms after Doctor Manhattan wins him the Vietnam War
examples: Starbuck in Kurt Vonnegut's novel Jailbird.
In Watchmen, this person serves five straight terms after Doctor Manhattan wins him a war.

clues.clue: Historian Rick Perlstein's biography Nixonland uses this man's Orthogonian-Franklin rivalry to argue he hated elites; historian Garry Wills instead called him "the last liberal" in Nixon Agonistes
examples: Name this book by Rick Perlstein that analyzes the "fracturing of America" during the 1960s through the rise of the title politician.
This historian labeled Nixon "the last liberal" in his book Nixon Agonistes.

clues.clue: On Rowan & Martin's Laugh-In in 1968, this politician said the show's catchphrase "Sock it to me?" in an attempt to appeal to younger voters
examples: Name this four-word phrase uttered by Richard Nixon on the show Rowan and Martin's Laugh-In in an attempt to appeal to younger voters before the 1968 election.

clues.clue: Rubber masks depicting this president's face were worn by the bank-robbing surfers in the poster for Point Break and, to conceal his identity, by psychiatrist John E. Fryer while testifying at a 1972 APA meeting on removing homosexuality from the DSM
examples: A man wearing one of these objects poses with peace signs held above his head in the poster for the film Point Break.
Fryer wore one of these objects to conceal his identity while testifying at a 1972 APA meeting over removing homosexuality from the DSM.

clues.clue: This president's presidential library and museum, in Yorba Linda, California, features a replica of the Oval Office; Duke, San Clemente, and Fullerton all rejected offers to host it
examples: name this complex in Yorba Linda, California that features a replica of the Oval Office and an exhibit on Watergate.
Duke, San Clemente, and Fullerton all rejected offers to host this place.

comprehensive_summary: Richard Nixon (born 1913) was the 37th President of the United States, a California Congressman and Senator, and Dwight Eisenhower's Vice President before that, nicknamed "Tricky Dick." He built his early career on aggressive anticommunist campaigning, unseating Jerry Voorhis for Congress, pursuing accused spy Alger Hiss, and winning a bitter 1950 Senate race against Helen Gahagan Douglas by branding her "pink right down to her underwear" on the "Pink Sheet" -- earning her nickname "The Pink Lady" as he became "Tricky Dick." As Eisenhower's running mate, he survived a funding scandal with the televised 1952 "Checkers Speech," and as Vice President he sparred with Nikita Khrushchev in the 1959 "Kitchen Debate" and survived a car-stoning mob in Caracas. In 1960, he lost the first televised presidential debates -- and the election -- to John F. Kennedy, partly due to his haggard appearance after injuring his knee on a car door. As president from 1969 to 1974, Nixon signed a wave of environmental legislation, creating the EPA and the Endangered Species Act while Congress overrode his vetoes of the Clean Air and Clean Water Acts; he also imposed wage-price controls, took the U.S. off the gold standard in the "Nixon Shock," and unsuccessfully proposed a guaranteed-income Family Assistance Plan. His most celebrated foreign-policy achievement was his 1972 trip to China, the first by a sitting U.S. president, where he met Mao Zedong and Zhou Enlai and produced the Shanghai Communique through Henry Kissinger's secret diplomacy. His conduct of the Vietnam War included a 1968 back-channel effort (via Anna Chennault) to sabotage Lyndon Johnson's Paris peace talks, his presidential policy of "Vietnamization" and appeals to the "Silent Majority," the secret invasion of Cambodia, an impulsive late-night visit to student protesters at the Lincoln Memorial, and the massive 1971 May Day protests. His presidency was ultimately destroyed by the Watergate scandal: after a break-in at the Democratic National Committee headquarters, secret White House tapes recorded him discussing a cover-up, his "enemies list" targeted critics like Jack Anderson and Arthur Schlesinger Jr., and the "Saturday Night Massacre" firing of special prosecutor Archibald Cox provoked national outrage. His successor special prosecutor Leon Jaworski won the Supreme Court case U.S. v. Nixon, forcing him to surrender tapes containing a notorious 18.5-minute gap, and Nixon resigned in August 1974 -- the only U.S. president ever to do so -- before Gerald Ford pardoned him via Proclamation 4311. Nixon's outsized life has generated an unusual body of art and satire, most famously John Adams's opera Nixon in China (in which his wife Pat Nixon is a major character), along with fictional appearances in works by Thomas Pynchon, Robert Coover, Kurt Vonnegut, and Alan Moore's Watchmen, Rick Perlstein's biography Nixonland, and the pop-culture staple of the rubber Nixon mask; his presidential library stands in Yorba Linda, California.
recursive_suggestions: John Adams's opera Nixon in China -- recurs constantly as its own answerline with dense musical/operatic clues (orchestration, Alice Goodman's libretto, Peter Sellars's staging, specific arias); substantially covered here for its Nixon-biographical content, but a Fine Arts deep-dive on the opera itself (separate from Nixon's own page) would need much more musical detail.

Pat Nixon -- recurs as her own answerline (both as historical figure and as the John Adams opera character); folded into the Legacy section here, but has enough independent clue presence (Peru earthquake relief, Venezuela incident, the opera role) to support a standalone First Lady page.

The Nixon Tapes / Watergate Tapes -- recur as their own tossup answerline (T8, T20, B21p1, B29p1) distinct from 'Richard Nixon'; substantially folded into the Watergate section here given how central they are, but could be split out.
Ford Pardoning Nixon -- recurs as its own answerline (T26, T28, B27p2); folded into the Watergate/Resignation section here as the natural coda to Nixon's story.
Helen Gahagan Douglas -- recurs with her own answerline presence (paired tossup T18) beyond her role as Nixon's 1950 opponent; a candidate for her own Figure page.

Nixon-Kennedy Debates -- recur as their own dense answerline (T1, T23, T29, B40) with debate-specific details (Addison's disease, Operation Mongoose, moderators); substantially folded in here but could support a standalone Event page alongside JFK's.

Rick Perlstein's Nixonland and the broader Orthogonian/Franklin framework -- recurs across multiple bonus parts as a historiographical lens; noted in Legacy but a fuller literature-of-Nixon treatment was out of scope here.

Ambiguity flag: 'Senator from New York' (T17, really about Daniel Patrick Moynihan's career) and 'U.S. Presidential debates' (T13, a general-topic tossup only tangentially touching Nixon) and 'the 1960s' (T7, a general decade tossup) were excluded as distinct/broader-topic answerlines.
Ambiguity flag: one bonus part (B31 Part 2, 'Jay Nixon') is about a wholly unrelated Missouri governor who merely shares the surname; excluded as namesake contamination.

Kissinger, Herblock, Rick Perlstein, Leon Jaworski, H.R. Haldeman -- all recur with dense clue clusters of their own as distinct historical/journalistic figures; their Nixon-relevant facts are folded in here, but each could support an independent Figure page.
